title: Budgeting With The 50/30/20 Rule
content: A simple starter budget splits after-tax income into needs, wants, and saving or debt payoff. It is not a strict law. Students can adapt the percentages when rent, tuition, or family costs are high.

title: Emergency Fund Comes Before Risk
content: Before investing aggressively, many people build a small emergency fund. A starter goal can be one month of necessary expenses, then three to six months over time. This reduces the chance of using debt when something unexpected happens.

title: Debt Avalanche And Snowball
content: Debt avalanche means paying extra toward the highest interest rate first. Debt snowball means paying the smallest balance first for motivation. Both require making minimum payments on all debts.

title: Compound Growth
content: Compounding means money can earn returns, and those returns can earn more returns. Time matters a lot, but future returns are uncertain and should never be guaranteed.

title: Risk Tolerance
content: Risk tolerance depends on time horizon, income stability, emotional comfort, and the importance of the goal. Short-term money usually needs lower risk than long-term money.

title: Diversification
content: Diversification means not relying on one company, one sector, or one asset. It can reduce some risks, but it does not remove all risk or guarantee profit.

title: SMART Money Goals
content: A useful goal is specific, measurable, achievable, relevant, and time-bound. Example: save $1,200 in 12 months for a laptop by setting aside $100 per month.

title: Student Money Habits
content: Students can start with tracking spending, avoiding unnecessary high-interest debt, setting a weekly fun-money limit, and automating small savings. The habit matters more than the starting amount.

title: Credit Score Basics
content: Credit scores are influenced by on-time payment history, credit utilization, account age, credit mix, and recent applications. Paying on time and keeping balances low are common starting points.

title: Needs Versus Wants
content: Needs are expenses required for basic life and commitments. Wants are optional or flexible. A budget works better when it does not delete all wants, but gives them a clear limit.

title: Sinking Funds
content: A sinking fund saves a small amount each month for a known future expense, such as textbooks, travel, car repairs, or gifts. It turns surprise costs into planned costs.

title: Financial Advice Boundary
content: An educational AI can explain concepts, compare options, and help create a draft plan. It should not claim to be a licensed advisor, guarantee returns, or give personalized buy/sell instructions.
